name: Argumentative Essay
description: Formal argument-driven essay with clear thesis, structured evidence, and addressed objections

voice.formality: high — no contractions, no colloquialisms, no first person unless discipline conventions allow it
voice.sentenceLength: medium-to-long — complex ideas earn complex sentences, but each clause must carry weight. Sentences containing logical operators (if-then, therefore, because) must not exceed 35 words.
voice.paragraphStyle: topic-sentence-led — every paragraph opens with a claim or transition that tells the reader what this paragraph will do
voice.rhetoricalStyle: logos-primary — arguments advance through evidence and logical structure, not appeals to emotion or authority alone. Closing sentences restate conclusions, never celebrate or editorialize.

voiceBehaviors: Uses declarative thesis statement in the opening paragraph — the reader knows the claim before body paragraphs begin
Avoids contractions entirely — 'do not' not 'don't', 'cannot' not 'can't'
Each paragraph advances exactly one claim with supporting evidence
Uses explicit logical connectives ('therefore', 'however', 'because', 'nevertheless') to make argument structure visible
Qualifies claims precisely — 'most studies show' not 'studies show', 'in three of four cases' not 'often'
Addresses counterarguments directly rather than ignoring them — a strong argument acknowledges the strongest objection
Avoids rhetorical questions — asserts and defends rather than asking
Maintains consistent register throughout — does not shift between casual and formal
Defines every technical term, theory name, or discipline-specific concept in plain language on first use — the reader should never need outside knowledge to follow the argument
Every example earns its place — if an example illustrates the same point as a previous example without adding new information, cut it

structure.introStyle: thesis-forward — context is brief, thesis appears within the first three sentences
structure.endingStyle: restate-and-broaden — restates thesis in light of evidence presented, then gestures at wider implications without overreaching

criteria.name: novelty
criteria.description: Thesis names the specific view it opposes and states why reasonable people hold that view before arguing against it. The essay takes a position that reasonable people could disagree with — not obvious, not merely descriptive.

criteria.name: clarity
criteria.description: Each sentence has exactly one parse. Every premise is supported before the next premise appears. Key concepts are introduced with a definition before being used in an argument. No sentence requires re-reading to determine what it claims.

criteria.name: structure
criteria.description: Thesis governs every paragraph. Each section transition states what the next section will do and why it follows from what came before. Body paragraphs follow logical order (not arbitrary). Conclusion follows from the body, not from general sentiment.

criteria.name: voice_preservation
criteria.description: Analytical register maintained throughout. The author's chosen level of certainty for each claim is preserved — hedges are not removed, assertions are not weakened. Author's specific argumentative choices (which objections to address, which evidence to foreground) are preserved.

criteria.name: audience_fit
criteria.description: Every technical term, theory name, or specialized concept is defined in plain language within two sentences of first use. Examples follow abstract claims within the same paragraph. Analogies draw from everyday experience, not from other technical domains. No unexplained jargon. No over-explaining of concepts the audience would find obvious.

criteria.name: concision
criteria.description: No paragraph exists solely to pad length. Evidence is sufficient, not exhaustive. Repetition of the thesis is strategic (intro and conclusion), not accidental.

criteria.name: factual_integrity
criteria.description: No invented citations, no fabricated evidence, no strengthened claims beyond what source material supports. Contested empirical claims include hedging language (suggests, indicates) rather than asserting as established fact. Qualifications in the original are preserved.
